title: Why the hippocampus shrinks under prolonged cortisol exposure
author: Kalesh
excerpt:
Your brain on cortisol 

 I remember the first time I learned that stress could literally shrink parts of your brain. I was sitting in a dimly lit lecture hall...
metaDescription:
Your brain on cortisol 

 I remember the first time I learned that stress could literally shrink parts of your brain. I was sitting in a dimly lit lecture...
htmlContent:
Your brain on cortisol

I remember the first time I learned that stress could literally shrink parts of your brain. I was sitting in a dimly lit lecture hall, maybe twenty-two years old, and the professor put up a slide showing two MRI scans side by side. One hippocampus looked full, healthy, almost plump. The other looked withered, like a dried raisin. Same age person. Different stress history. I felt my stomach drop. Because I knew, even then, which scan looked more like mine.

Here's the thing. That shrinking isn't some abstract metaphor. It's not a poetic way of saying you feel foggy or forgetful. It's a real, measurable, physical change in the structure of your brain. And the primary driver of that change is cortisol, the stress hormone that your body pumps out when it thinks you're in danger. The problem is, when you live with complex trauma, your body thinks you're in danger pretty much all the time. Even when you're safe. Even when you're asleep. Even when you're trying to relax.

So the cortisol keeps flowing. Day after day. Year after year. And your hippocampus, that seahorse-shaped structure deep in your temporal lobe that helps you form new memories and regulate your emotions, starts to pay the price. Stay with me here, because I want to walk you through exactly how this happens. Not to scare you. But because understanding the mechanism can sometimes help you feel less broken. Less like something is wrong with you. More like your brain is doing exactly what it evolved to do in response to an impossible situation.

What the hippocampus actually does

Let's start with what this little structure is for. The hippocampus is one of the most densely packed regions of glucocorticoid receptors in your entire brain. Glucocorticoid receptors are basically docking stations for cortisol. They're designed to receive the stress signal and help your brain adapt. But here's the catch. They're designed for short bursts of cortisol. The kind you get when you're running from a predator. Not the kind you get when you're living in a chronically unsafe home, or working a job that grinds you down, or carrying the weight of unprocessed trauma for decades.

When cortisol binds to those receptors in a healthy, short-term way, it actually helps you form stronger memories of the event. That's adaptive. You want to remember where the tiger lives so you can avoid it next time. But when cortisol binds to those receptors chronically, something different starts to happen. The receptors themselves start to downregulate. They become less sensitive. Your brain is basically trying to protect itself from the constant signal. But the cortisol keeps coming. And without enough functional receptors to handle it, the neurons in your hippocampus start to suffer.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, has written extensively about how unresolved trauma keeps the nervous system locked in a state of threat response. When your body can't complete the natural cycle of arousal and discharge that comes with a real threat, the stress chemicals stay in your system. Your hippocampus, designed to process and file away experiences, never gets the all-clear signal. So it stays in a kind of limbo. Trying to work. Trying to do its job. But drowning in cortisol.

Think about what that feels like in real life. You walk into a room and forget why. You lose your train of thought mid-sentence. You can't remember what you did yesterday, or last week, or last year. You feel like your memory is Swiss cheese. And then you blame yourself. You call yourself stupid. You worry you're developing early dementia. But what's actually happening is that your hippocampus is struggling to do its job because the chemical environment it's operating in is toxic to it. Wild, right? Your own stress response, the thing that's supposed to keep you alive, is slowly damaging the part of your brain that helps you make sense of your life.

The mechanism of shrinkage

So how does cortisol actually shrink the hippocampus? It's not like the brain cells just evaporate. It's more than that, and honestly, a little more hopeful. Because understanding the mechanism gives you some insight into what might help.

First, chronic cortisol exposure suppresses neurogenesis. That's the process by which your brain grows new neurons. For a long time, scientists thought adult brains couldn't grow new neurons at all. But we now know that the hippocampus is one of the few regions where neurogenesis continues throughout life. New neurons are born there every day. They migrate into place. They integrate into existing circuits. This is part of how you learn new things and form new memories. But cortisol shuts that process down. It tells the brain, basically, "Don't invest resources in growing new cells right now. We're in survival mode. We need to conserve energy." So the stem cells that would have become new neurons either die or go dormant.

Second, cortisol causes dendritic retraction. Neurons have these branching structures called dendrites that reach out and connect with other neurons. Think of them like tree branches. Under chronic cortisol exposure, those branches start to pull back. They get shorter. They have fewer connections. The neuron is still alive, but it's less connected. It's isolated. This is part of why you can feel so disconnected from yourself and others when you're in a chronic stress state. Your brain's physical architecture is literally pulling away from connection.

Third, and this is the scariest one, cortisol can actually trigger apoptosis in hippocampal neurons. Apoptosis is programmed cell death. The cell basically self-destructs. This happens when the cortisol exposure is severe enough and prolonged enough. The cells that die are gone. They don't come back. And that's part of what shows up on those MRI scans as visible shrinkage. Bessel van der Kolk, who wrote extensively about this in his work with trauma survivors, has shown that people with PTSD have measurably smaller hippocampi than people without trauma histories. The difference can be as much as 8 to 10 percent. That's not nothing.

I want to pause here and say something direct. If you're reading this and feeling a wave of despair, I get it. It's terrifying to think that your own biology is working against you. That the very system designed to keep you alive is slowly damaging the organ that makes you who you are. But here's the thing I need you to hold onto. The brain is also incredibly plastic. It can change. It can adapt. And while we can't bring back dead neurons, we can absolutely support the conditions that allow the remaining ones to grow, connect, and thrive.

What helps protect the hippocampus

This is where the science gets genuinely hopeful, if you can stay with me. Because researchers have identified several factors that seem to protect the hippocampus from cortisol damage, and even promote recovery in people who have experienced significant stress.

One of the most well-studied is aerobic exercise. I know, I know. You've heard it a thousand times. But the reason it keeps coming up is that it works. Exercise increases brain-derived neurotrophic factor, or BDNF, which is basically fertilizer for your brain. It promotes neurogenesis in the hippocampus. It helps those stem cells wake up and start making new neurons again. It also helps regulate the HPA axis, the system that controls cortisol release. So you're simultaneously lowering the stress signal and giving your brain the tools to repair itself. A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link), which goes into detail about how movement and body-based practices can rewire the traumatized brain.

Another factor is social connection. This one is harder when you're in a trauma state, because your nervous system is telling you that people are dangerous. But safe, attuned connection with others has been shown to buffer the effects of cortisol on the hippocampus. When you feel seen and understood by another person, your body releases oxytocin, which actually counteracts some of cortisol's damaging effects. It's not about having a huge social circle. It's about having even one person who gets it. One person who doesn't need you to explain yourself. A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link) by Bruce Perry and Oprah Winfrey, which explores how relationships can heal the developing brain.

Sleep is another critical factor. During deep sleep, your brain clears out metabolic waste and consolidates memories. It also regulates cortisol. But trauma often disrupts sleep, creating a vicious cycle. Poor sleep leads to higher cortisol. Higher cortisol leads to worse sleep. And your hippocampus, already struggling, gets caught in the middle. If you can find ways to improve your sleep hygiene, even incrementally, you're giving your hippocampus a fighting chance.

And then there's therapy. Specifically, therapies that target the nervous system directly. Somatic approaches. EMDR. Sensorimotor psychotherapy. These modalities don't just talk about the trauma. They help your body complete the stress response cycles that got stuck. They help your nervous system learn that the danger is over. For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link) by Peter Levine, which lays out the theory behind how trauma gets trapped in the body and how to release it.

What recovery actually looks like

I want to be honest with you about what recovery from hippocampal shrinkage looks like. It's not a straight line. It's not a quick fix. And it's not about your brain going back to some perfect pre-trauma state. Because that version of your brain, the one that never experienced what you experienced, doesn't exist anymore. And honestly, that's okay.

What recovery looks like is your brain finding new ways to compensate. New pathways. New connections. Your hippocampus might not regain every lost neuron, but the neurons that remain can grow stronger connections. They can recruit other brain regions to help with memory and emotional regulation. Your brain is incredibly resourceful. It wants to heal. It's been working so hard to keep you alive, and it will work just as hard to help you thrive if you give it the right conditions.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed polyvagal theory, talks about the importance of neuroception. That's your nervous system's ability to detect safety or danger without conscious thought. When your neuroception is calibrated to danger, your hippocampus gets flooded with cortisol. But as you heal, your neuroception can shift. Your body starts to recognize that some things are safe. Some people are safe. Some moments are safe. And that shift, gradual as it is, changes the chemical environment your hippocampus lives in. A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link) by Deb Dana, which offers exercises for working with the nervous system.

I'm not going to tell you that your brain will be exactly the same as someone who never experienced trauma. It won't be. But your brain will be yours. It will have adapted to survive conditions that would have broken someone else. And that adaptation, while painful, also carries wisdom. Your hippocampus, even in its shrunken state, holds the story of what you've been through. And that story matters. It's not just damage. It's evidence of your endurance.

So if you're sitting here wondering whether your brain is permanently broken, I want you to consider this. Your brain is not broken. It's wounded. And wounds, if given the right conditions, can heal. Not without scars. But with new strength where the scar tissue forms. Your hippocampus is not gone. It's waiting. Waiting for the cortisol to recede. Waiting for safety to arrive. Waiting for you to find the resources that help it grow again.

Notice what just happened in your body when you read that. Did your shoulders drop a little? Did your breath get easier? That's your nervous system responding to the possibility of safety. That's your brain, even now, looking for a way back to health. Trust that. It knows what it's doing.

Healing Journey Resources

  • The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) - Bessel van der Kolk's seminal work on how trauma reshapes the brain and body.
  • What Happened to You? (paid link) - Bruce Perry and Oprah Winfrey's compassionate exploration of trauma and healing through relationships.
  • Waking the Tiger (paid link) - Peter Levine's foundational text on somatic approaches to trauma release.
  • The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link) - Deb Dana's practical guide to working with the nervous system in clinical settings.

As an Amazon Associate, I earn from qualifying purchases.
